name: Classical and Conventional Phillips-Type Curves, 1948-1981

book_quotes.quote: The contrast between the classical and conventional Phillips curves becomes even starker when we look separately at the pre- and post-neoliberal eras of 1948-1981 and 1982-2010, respectively.
Figure 15.8 Classical and Conventional Phillip-Type Curves, 1948-1981

primary_source.publication: Same as S1505 (Inflation pi from BEA NIPA T1.1.4; sigma from BEA T5.3.5 / T1.16; uL from BLS)
primary_source.table_or_series_id: Subset of S1505 series, restricted to years 1948-1981 (pre-Volcker / pre-neoliberal era)

formula: Sub-period restriction of S1505 to 1948-1981. Variables: pi, (1 - sigma'), uL.

components.label: Inflation rate (pi), 1948-1981
components.source: BEA NIPA T1.1.4 % change in GDP deflator

components.label: Growth-utilization rate (sigma), 1948-1981
components.source: BEA NIPA T5.3.5 line 2 / T1.16 line 2 (Handfas 2012)

extension_candidates.subseries_label: Same as S1505; this sub-period series will not be re-extended (historical break)
concerns: This series is defined as a historical sub-period for analytical contrast; no forward extension is meaningful.

methodology_notes: Pre-neoliberal sub-period of S1505 — same data, restricted to 1948-1981.
Shaikh argues this period shows a clear downward-sloping classical curve and is the period in which Phillips's original empirical relation roughly held.
Endpoint 1981 chosen as the last year before the Volcker-shock-initiated regime change (1982+ becomes Fig 15.9 = S1507).
open_questions: None additional beyond S1505's open questions.

review_history.notes: CD2 S084 ported; quotes verified against PDF p.708 and Appendix 15.1 p.896
